coredata.dc:title: Advances in safety of lithium-ion batteries for energy storage: Hazard characteristics and active suppression techniques
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Shenzhen City Clean Energy Research Institute, Shenzhen University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Shenzhen City Clean Energy Research Institute, Shenzhen University.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yan
Yu, Tianmin
Chen, Jie
Gao, Baobin
Yu, Mingqiao
Zhu, Jiateng
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are widely regarded as established energy storage devices owing to their high energy density, extended cycling life, and rapid charging capabilities. Nevertheless, the stark contrast between the frequent incidence of safety incidents in battery energy storage systems (BESS) and the substantial demand within the energy storage market has become increasingly prominent. Firstly, despite the escalating demand for energy density in BESS, in-depth understanding of thermal runaway (TR) in large-capacity LIBs and the associated risks posed by battery venting gases (BVG) remains elusive. Secondly, the absence of consensus regarding the selection of fire extinguishing agents and the development of fire suppression strategies for BESS has resulted in prevailing technological inadequacies in active suppression measures. This manuscript comprehensively reviews the characteristics and associated influencing factors of the four hazard stages of TR, TR propagation, BVG accumulation, and fire (BVG combustion and explosion), particularly focusing on the spatial characteristics of energy storage. Combining the above analysis, the suppression mechanisms, effects, and applicable hazard stages of extinguishing agents are analyzed, and the positive effects of fire suppression strategies are discussed. Additionally, the experimental research, mathematical calculation and numerical simulation methods pertaining to the combustion and explosion characteristics are summarized, alongside offering corresponding research suggestions and prospects aimed at fostering advancements in BESS safety.

Lithium-ion battery

Evolution process of TR: Evolution stage, characteristic temperature and characteristic phenomenon.

row.entry: Chemical reactions inside the battery are minimal, with heating primarily resulting from external sources.

When T s
b reaches T 1, the SEI c film decomposes, exposing the anode and enabling Li precipitation and reaction with the electrolyte. The temperature rise rate rapidly reach 1 ​°C/s [29,30], attributed as the primary cause of the voltage drop [31].

Upon reaching T SC, the ISC occurs, releasing a substantial amount of stored electrical energy, followed by obvious battery bulge, SV opening and venting [25], leading to T s reaching the T 2. At this stage, combustible gas vents from the SV due to pressure differences, providing sufficient ignition energy to form a jet flame.

The peak temperature rise rate is close to 10 ​°C/s, with electrolyte pyrolysis commencing when T s reaches between 200 ​°C and 280 ​°C [32]. Upon reaching temperatures between 240 ​°C and 350 ​°C, residual Li+ of the anode reacts with the binder, and O2 generated by the decomposition of the LFP cathode reacts with the electrolyte solvent to release heat [33], ultimately causing T s reach the T 3 [25].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Influencing factors of TR and TRP process.

row.entry: Cause or phenomenon of influencing factors
Higher SOC levels correlate with a more pronounced promotion of TR.

As SOC increases, the thermal stability of the cathode material deteriorates. Meanwhile, with the increase of anode Li+ content, the reaction between the anode and the electrolyte intensifies, resulting in a lower TR triggering temperature [63].
TR severity increases with higher capacity.
Higher capacity causes the breakage of high-energy P=O bonds, leading to the release of increased amounts of O2 and potent oxidizing substances [64].
A lower SOH corresponds to a more pronounced promoting effect [65], with the effect being particularly evident when the SOH drops below 80 ​% [50].

The Li+ content precipitated by the anode increases with the decrease of SOH. Additionally, lower SOH levels correspond to increased battery aging, more significant capacity decay, poorer thermal stability, and lower T 2 [65].

Reduced ambient pressure inhibits TR and TRP.
Higher ambient temperatures promote TR and TRP.
TRP occurs faster in air atmosphere than N2 atmosphere.

Reduced ambient pressure decreases TR trigger time, T s, mass loss rate, and HRR [66,67], as well as TRP speed [68]. Higher ambient temperatures lead to an increase in surface temperature rise rate and post-TR side reaction heat production [65].
In air atmosphere, the jet flame duration is prolonged, and TRP speed is about 8 times faster compared to N2 atmosphere [69].
Increasing the charging rate promotes both TR and TRP of the LIBs.

Increasing the charging rate from 0.5C to 3C results in lower voltage and ignition temperature, as well as higher maximum temperature and HRR are higher [70]. Higher charging rates significantly increase TRP speed [71].
TRP speed and TR hazard increase simultaneously in parallel state.
Enhanced heat conduction occurs between batteries [72]. The transmission process generates higher joule heat [73].
Small-sized battery exhibit higher TR risk, while TRP is lower.
Small-sized battery indicate higher temperature peak [74], but exhibit relatively slower TRP speed [75].
Among circular and cavity SVs, elliptical SV exhibits the lowest risk.
Circular SV has a significantly higher opening pressure compared to the other two types. Cavity SV produces acceleration upon opening, resulting in the highest airflow velocity [76].

list-item.para: At about 200 ​°C, LiPF6 accelerates pyrolysis, yielding Lewis acid PF5, which subsequently reacts with water to generate HF. LiPF6 hydrolyzes to HF at lower temperatures [99,100].

Further, DEC e and EMC f react with PF5 [101,102], with HF participating in subsequent reactions [98]. EC g , DEC, DMC h , and EMC have boiling points of 248 ​°C, 91 ​°C, 127 ​°C, and 107 ​°C, respectively, indicating significant electrolyte solvent evaporation before this stage [34].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The other mathematical calculations for FL and EL of BVG.

row.entry: The Jones method, each combustible gas is mixed with a dilute gas like CO2 and the corresponding values from the Jones table are incorporated into the L-C formula for computation.
While the error is minor, the specific dilution may introduce uncertainty into the outcomes.
Bounaceur's modified CAFT method calculates the concentration of BVG mixture (i.e., LFL) requires to attain the adiabatic flame temperature by first determining the mixing adiabatic temperature.
The extent of dilution is assessed through the dilution ratio (DR), determined by substituting the newly synthesized gas mixture as an independent component into the L-C equation.
The suppression effect of CO2 on FL can be measured quantitatively.

The energy liberated by the combustible gas numerically equals the aggregate heat dissipated by both the combustible gas and air within the system. This principle applies to the reaction limit state at UEL, so UEL can be calculated thermodynamically.
The scenario of incomplete gas reaction near the UEL is taken into account.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Suppression effects of water-based extinguishing agents on large-capacity LIBs.

row.entry: Immediately spraying FWM after opening the SV can entirely halt TR evolution, with the battery side temperature ranging between 122.0 ​°C and 149.7 ​°C [187].
Although spraying FWM immediately after the SV is opened fails to suppress TR evolution, it reduces the average peak temperature of the battery T s by 133 ​°C [188].

Releasing FWM at 6 ​MPa before the SV opening, upon SV opening, 3 ​min post-SV opening, upon TR occurrence, and when T s reaches 400 ​°C, respectively. FWM effectively suppress TR evolution in the first three stages. However, upon TR occurrence, FWM face the flame so fiercely that rapid extinguishing is not feasible [189].
Releasing FWM after SV opening fails to suppress TR evolution, yet it reduces the T s peak to 122.1 ​°C. When TR occurs, the peak T s after FWM release still reaches 443.0 ​°C [190].
Releasing FWM after TR, extinguishing the fire and interdicting the TRP [49].
SV opens for 5 ​s and releases FWM, extinguishing the fire and interdicting the TRP. At the end of the release, T s drops below 100 ​°C [191].
Releasing FWM after the first battery-TR, in a confined space, with a 0.5 ​mm aerogel insulation panel to extinguish the fire and interdict TRP [192].

In the severe TR stage, releasing extinguishing agent quickly extinguishes the fire, reducing all measurement points T s reduced to 100 ​°C and the smoke temperature relative to the pure FWM reduction. F-500 modified FWM suppression effect is better [179].
A small amount of hydrogel can effectively extinguish the fire and suppress TR and TRP, yielding significantly better results than pure water under specific spray timing [183].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Suppression effects of gaseous, solid and multi-phase extinguishing agents on large-capacity LIBs.

row.entry: Even if the fire is temporarily extinguished, the battery often re-ignites and cannot be consistently suppressed [193,204].
Releasing HFC-227ea after the SV opens fails to suppress TR evolution and is ineffective in quickly extinguishing the fire [188].
Releasing Novec 1230 30 ​s after jet flame can extinguish the fire and effectively prevent TRP within 16 ​s [205].
Releasing Novec 1230 after the first battery-TR, it can extinguish the fire in confined space, but even with 0.5 ​mm aerogel insulation panels, the TRP can't be suppressed [192].
A small amount of LN can suppress the battery warming before the SV opens, and a large amount of LN can suppress TR after it occurs [206].
In confined spaces, injecting LN after TR occurs, using 11–18 ​kg of LN, can suppress both TR evolution and TRP within the module [160].
Injecting LN during voltage drops completely suppresses TR and TRP [199].
While the fire can be extinguished, effective cooling is not achieved, leading to potential re-ignition [207].
Releasing aerosol after about 2 ​min of jet flame and extensive batteries combustion also results in re-ignition after extinguishing the fire [204].

head.ce:title: Advances in safety of lithium-ion batteries for energy storage: Hazard characteristics and active suppression techniques

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are widely regarded as established energy storage devices owing to their high energy density, extended cycling life, and rapid charging capabilities. Nevertheless, the stark contrast between the frequent incidence of safety incidents in battery energy storage systems (BESS) and the substantial demand within the energy storage market has become increasingly prominent. Firstly, despite the escalating demand for energy density in BESS, in-depth understanding of thermal runaway (TR) in large-capacity LIBs and the associated risks posed by battery venting gases (BVG) remains elusive. Secondly, the absence of consensus regarding the selection of fire extinguishing agents and the development of fire suppression strategies for BESS has resulted in prevailing technological inadequacies in active suppression measures. This manuscript comprehensively reviews the characteristics and associated influencing factors of the four hazard stages of TR, TR propagation, BVG accumulation, and fire (BVG combustion and explosion), particularly focusing on the spatial characteristics of energy storage. Combining the above analysis, the suppression mechanisms, effects, and applicable hazard stages of extinguishing agents are analyzed, and the positive effects of fire suppression strategies are discussed. Additionally, the experimental research, mathematical calculation and numerical simulation methods pertaining to the combustion and explosion characteristics are summarized, alongside offering corresponding research suggestions and prospects aimed at fostering advancements in BESS safety.

The final line of defense for battery energy storage system: the full-process active suppression techniques and suppression mechanism for the characteristics of four hazardous phases of lithium-ion battery.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The depletion of fossil energy resources and the inadequacies in energy structure have emerged as pressing issues, serving as significant impediments to the sustainable progress of society [1]. Battery energy storage systems (BESS) represent pivotal technologies facilitating energy transformation, extensively employed across power supply, grid, and user domains, which can realize the decoupling between power generation and electricity consumption in the power system, thereby enhancing the efficiency of renewable energy utilization [2,3]. In the light of its advantages of low self-discharge rate, long cycling life and high specific energy, lithium-ion battery (LIBs) is currently at the forefront of energy storage carrier [4,5]. However, as the demand for energy density in BESS rises, large-capacity batteries of 280–320 Ah are widely used, heightens the risk of thermal runaway (TR) [6,7]. In addition, battery venting gas (BVG) generated during TR contains large quantities of flammable gases such as H2 and CO, which can violently explode in the confined or enclosed space of BESS, posing severe risks of bodily harm [8,9]. Safety accidents involving BESS and their production chains have been prevalent in countries such as Korea, the United States, and China, leading to casualties and significant property damage (Fig. 1 ). In May 2024, it took two weeks to extinguish a fire at the Gateway energy storage plant in California, United States. In June of the same year, a fire broke out in a LIBs factory in northern South Korea, resulting in over 20 fatalities. These incidents present substantial challenges to the promotion of BESS, particularly in distributed energy storage scenarios that are in closer proximity to people [10–16]. The occurrence of BESS safety accidents often leads to a larger negative social impact. Therefore, the dilemma of the high energy density and low safety seriously impedes the large-scale promotion of BESS.

Distinguished from conventional fires, the complexity of LIBs fire lies in that it contains characteristics of ABCE class fires, with the battery itself supplying all three elements of fire. In BESS, the internal chemistry and ohmic internal resistance heat generation during charging and discharging may lead to TR [17]. Once TR occurs, the heat of the chemical reaction and joule heat generated by the internal short-circuit (ISC) accumulate rapidly [18], making the battery a high-temperature heat source. Meanwhile, the electrical sparks generated by the friction process as the products of the internal reaction (metal particles, BVG, etc.) are ejected out of the safety valve (SV), also provide the ignition energy [19]. Notably, these products have high exothermic capacities. For instance, a fully charged 68 Ah lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery has a normalized heat release rate (HRR) during combustion comparable to gasoline and higher than many other combustibles, including fuel oil [20]. O2 is sourced from the environment and is also generated during the decomposition of the battery's cathode material, where the O2 produced from this decomposition participates in the internal reaction [21,22], further contributing to the battery's heating. However, in BESS, fire hazards are only one aspect of safety concerns, thermal runaway propagation (TRP), toxic gas diffusion, and explosions of combustible gases also present significant risks. Therefore, it is necessary to systematically understand the hazard characteristics of large-capacity LIBs and develop active suppression technologies that are highly targeted, efficient, and integrable.

Recent years have witnessed numerous review articles addressing the hazardous characteristics and suppression techniques of LIBs. This manuscript primarily focuses on large-capacity LFP or ternary lithium batteries, commonly employed in BESS applications [23]. The TR and TRP processes of LIBs, as well as the generation mechanism, toxicity, combustion and explosion characteristics of BVG are summarized in combination with the spatial characteristics of BESS. On this basis, the suppression mechanisms, effects and applicable hazard stages of existing active suppression technologies such as fire extinguishing agents and strategies are analyzed. Meanwhile, the influencing factors of the above characteristics are discussed in detail, and the experimental research and numerical simulation methods of combustion and explosion characteristics are summarized, expecting to offer valuable references for BESS safety research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TR and TRP processes of large-capacity LIBs

ce:section.ce:para: Research on the TR evolution process could aid in evaluating the security performance of LIBs and identifying optimal early warning and suppression phases. This section introduces the TR process of commercial 280 Ah LFP battery from two dimensions of TR heat transfer mechanism and evolution process. Fig. 2 (a, c) illustrates the electrothermal coupling mechanism of battery-TR under puncture condition and the thermal-resistance network model in response to TR evolution process. When the first jelly roll is penetrated by a nail, the heat generated by the internal large-scale ISC resulting from separator melting triggers TR in LIBs. At this stage, the heat transfer process is mainly reflected in the heat conduction between the nail and the first jelly roll, along with heat convection and radiation with the environment. By now, friction from the pinhole and smoke flow during the venting process may result in fire. Additionally, the second jelly roll may undergo TR rapidly due to heating from the first jelly roll, driven by temperature differences [24]. Under side heating conditions, Zhang et al. [25] established a thermodynamic TR prediction model based on Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) testing, offering quantitative analysis of temperature distribution during overheating (Fig. 2d). The results revealed a pronounced longitudinal temperature gradient, rather than a horizontal gradient, within the battery. The maximum internal temperature reached 681 ​°C. Moreover, the second jelly roll enters the TR faster due to prolonged preheating. As the number of jelly rolls increases, the TR process inside the battery is accelerated [26].

Research on evolutionary process of TR is more adequate. Feng et al. [27] identified three characteristic temperatures {T 1 (The self-heating temperature), T 2 (TR trigger temperature), T 3 (Maximum temperature of TR)}, corresponding to three key TR nodes, and divided TR evolution into four stages [28], as depicted in Fig. 2e and Table 1 .

Distinguish from the above understanding of characteristic temperature, large-capacity LFP batteries exhibit lower characteristic temperatures during overcharging tests [34]. This is attributed to the rapid overcharging-induced formation of large amounts of lithium dendrites on the anode surface. T 1 is reduced to 60 ​°C or lower, bringing forward the TR stage [19], posing a significant challenge to the temperature warning system. In summary, higher T 1 and T 2 values indicate greater battery safety, whereas T 3 is on the contrary, and T 2 serves as the critical parameter for evaluating the thermal safety performance of the battery, determining whether it enters the TR state.
Illustrated in Fig. 2f, when TR occurs and is not effectively suppressed, TRP ensues. Moreover, TRP exhibits differences under various spatial characteristics.

Research on open-space TRP is significant for elucidating its fundamental process. Under overheating conditions, the energy flow distribution in a module comprising 280 Ah LFP batteries allocates more than 75 ​% of energy to heating the battery itself (Q ge), approximately 20 ​% is carried out by ejecta (Q vent), and only about 5–7 ​% is transferred to the next battery [35]. Bottom and side surface heating is higher than the large surface heating, and the overall temperature of the battery in the first two states is higher. Consequently, the TRP speed is about 7 times that of the latter (up to 2.035 ​mm/s) [36]. Under penetration conditions, 12 ​% of the heat released by the TR in a module of 6 ​× ​25 Ah NCM batteries is sufficient to trigger TRP. Heat conduction (Q cod) through the outer shell of the battery primarily triggers adjacent battery-TR, rather than the heat radiation of flame (Q rad,f) and heat convection (Q conv) [37]. Under overcharging conditions, the total heat release (THR) from a module comprising two 38Ah NCM batteries can surpass 3000 ​kJ (with the overcharged battery THR exceeding 2000 ​kJ), more than doubling that of overheating conditions [38]. It is noteworthy that a high-state of charge (SOC) battery significantly expands post-TR, reducing the contact surface volume with adjacent batteries (resulting in decreased Q cod). delaying the TRP time, which will lead to heat accumulation, intensifying subsequent TR processes [28,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: TRP process in confined and enclosed spaces
ce:section.ce:para: Confined and enclosed spaces closely resemble the actual spatial characteristics of BESS, leading to a more intricate heat transfer path during the TRP process (Fig. 2f). The maximum radiant heat flux generated by the jet flame following battery-TR in confined space is about 4 times greater than that in the open space [40]. Upon contact with the top plate, the flame area expands, rebounds towards the battery surface, and significantly increases T s by promoting Q rad,f, thus enhancing the TRP process. This facilitation effect intensifies as the distance between the top plate and the battery surface diminishes, contributing over 50 ​% of the heat to the TRP process [41], which indicates that the heat radiation of flame is one of the main heat transfer paths in confined space. In an enclosed space, the jet flame is extinguished due to limited due to the limited O2 content. However, Q cod and Q rad inside the module increase while Q conv decrease due to hindered heat and mass transfer between high-temperature substances and external air. The internal temperature of the module, the maximum temperature of the battery and the heat flux between adjacent batteries will be higher, and the TRP speed will be accelerated [42]. Furthermore, the ambient temperature required to trigger TR decreases as the number of batteries increases [43], thereby escalating the risk of TRP [44]. Li et al. [45] concluded that after TRP occurs in a closed module composed of 12 ​× ​71 Ah NCM811 batteries, a high temperature of 1370 ​°C will be generated in the central area of the module, and the flow of high-temperature smoke changes the TRP path. The effect of smoke is contributed to the increase of radiant heat flow due to higher smoke yield [46]. In a word, confined and enclosed space can increase the risk of TRP, and the influence of jet flame and high-temperature smoke on the heat transfer process can no longer be ignored. The ability to block the heat transfer process of large-capacity battery module TRP in confined and enclosed space should be one of the important criteria to evaluate the effectiveness of battery thermal management system (BTMS) and fire extinguishing system.

While previous studies have extensively explored horizontal TRP paths, less emphasis has been placed on longitudinal TRP paths. Fang et al. [47] highlighted that heat radiation and heat convection from the bottom battery combustion flame are the primary factors driving TRP in longitudinally arranged battery configurations. In LFP batteries modules, the jet flame from the lower-layer monomer battery inadequately induces to TR in the upper module [48]. However, the jet flame and high-temperature smoke generated by TR in the lower module are sufficient to trigger TR in the upper module [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influencing factors of TR and TRP process
ce:section.ce:para: Batteries from different technological routes exhibit fundamental differences in terms of TR hazards. In terms of different cathode materials, with the risk hierarchy being LFP ​< ​LiMnO4 (LMO) ​< ​LiNixCoyMnzO2 (NCM) ​< ​LiCoO2 (LCO) [50,51]. Additionally, higher Ni content in NCM batteries correlates with increased risk [52,53]. Taking the LFP and NCM batteries used most by BESS as an example, because the olivine structure of the LFP possesses stable P = O bonds, requiring higher temperatures for decomposition compared to NCM batteries. Consequently, the TR risk is relatively low [54,55]. Regarding the TR phenomenon, NCM battery exhibit higher T s and fierce jet flame, whereas LFP batter necessitate an external ignition source for jet flame production [56]. Here, it is essential to address solid electrolyte batteries (SEBs), which have garnered significant attention due to their high energy density [57]. Compared to the flammability issues of liquid electrolytes (LEs), nonflammable solid-state electrolytes (SEs) are more thermally stable and possess high mechanical strength, limiting the formation of lithium dendrites [58]. However, recent studies on the thermal characterization of SEBs have shown that various SEBs still undergo TR with unexpected thermal hazards. Under overheating conditions, sulfide SEBs (Li3PS4 electrolyte) have a higher T 1 (approximately 250 ​°C) compared to LIBs, demonstrating their superior thermal stability. Unfortunately, sulfide SEBs exhibit a significantly shorter time from T 1 to T 2, the peak temperature T 3 is close to 800 ​°C, and producing more than three times the total heat compared to LIBs [59]. Rui et al. [60] conducted a detailed study of the thermal properties of four sulfide SEs (the glass-ceramic state of Li7P3S11, Li3PS4, and crystalline Li6PS5Cl, Li10GeP12S2) and found that the total heat production of all four SEs was higher than that of LEs by 900 ​%. Distinguishing the TR mechanism of LIBs from SEBs, the TR mechanism of SEBs primarily involves gas-solid and solid-solid reaction pathways. The former involves the reaction of highly reactive metallic lithium with O2 in high-temperature-activated SEs or O2 generated by the cathodic phase transition. This reaction produces a large amount of heat, triggering TR [61]. The latter involves the reaction of the electrolyte with cathodic decomposition products at higher temperatures, with almost no involvement of O2 [60]. This explains the superior thermal stability of crystalline electrolytes over glass-ceramic electrolytes, but a more in-depth comparative study is needed to elucidate the reasons for the greater heat release from SEs compared to LEs.

Different methods of abuse primarily impact the early stages of TRP. Among these, overcharging significantly amplifies the hazards in this stage [36,62]. Moreover, factors such as SOC, state of health (SOH), ambient temperature and pressure all play an important role, as outlined in Table 2 .

Analyzing the composition and content of BVG is the pre-phase basic work for assessing gas toxicity, combustion and explosion characteristics. Numerous studies have demonstrated that BVG components in various LIBs include H2, CO, CO2, CH4, C2H4, C2H6, etc. [77–79], and the content of various gas components determines the risk of TR occurrence in different batteries.

The constant volume pressure vessel can simulate various abuse methods and record T s and environmental pressure following TR occurrence [80], further through the Gas chromatograph (GC), Gas chromatography–Mass spectrometry (GC - MS, which can provide additional detection of electrolyte solvent vapor), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, capable of real-time detection of SO2, NOx, etc.), Hydrogen probe, Raman spectroscopy (for real-time monitoring), Ionization chromatograph (IC, for detecting HCl, etc.) to determine the gas components [81–83]. Among them, Zhang et al. [83] integrated the Raman spectroscopy system developed by them with GC - MS to realize real-time and in-situ detection of 6 kinds of gases and electrolyte vapors of BVG. To ensure comparability of BVG component content, this paper selected similar experimental methods as reference, and summarized the BVG components and percentage of LFP and NCM batteries, as shown in Table 3 .

As shown in Table 3, cathode materials wield significant influence over BVG composition and content. An increase in Ni content leads to a higher concentration of combustible gases in NCM batteries, especially H2. Additionally, BVG from LFP batteries contains more H2 compared to NCM batteries. Obvious influence from SOC has been clearly introduced, when SOC exceeds 50 ​%, the proportion of combustible gases, especially H2, increases significantly, while the share of CO2 diminishes notably. The study of Yang et al. [90] further demonstrated that the total molar mass of BVG increases steadily with rising SOC. Interestingly, there is a notable disparity in gas composition between NCM523 batteries module and monomer battery, the proportion of BVG components, especially H2 and CO, is significantly higher in the former, yet the reason for this disparity remains unexplained.

Additionally, the method of abuse employed significantly influences BVG composition. The large surface heating method predominantly results in elevated proportion of H2, while CO is relatively minimal. Conversely, the puncture method leads to higher CO proportion and lower H2. In the oven heating method, the total proportion of CO and H2 is the lowest, CO2 is the highest, but C2H4 is about twice that of other triggering modes. In the overcharging method, the total proportion of CO and H2 is the largest, with CO2 being the smallest (Fig. 3 a) [80]. Increasing the charging rate will further increase the risk. In the range of 1C–2C, the proportion of H2 is positively correlated with the charging rate, while the opposite is true for CO2, with the proportion of H2 reaching 60 ​% at a charging rate of 2C (Fig. 3b) [91]. To sum up, high-rate overcharging triggers high-SOC state battery-TR to generate more combustible gas, primarily H2. Therefore, a low-SOC should be maintained during LIBs storage and transportation.

Studying the generation mechanism of BVG is highly significant for analyzing the key reaction steps of TR and the impact of gas on the TR process. Wang et al. [92] made an important discovery that timely elimination of reducing gas components allows for early control of TR. This section summarizes the battery-TR process described in Section 2.1 to elucidate the relationship between the TR process and BVG generation mechanism, illustrated in Fig. 4
and Table 4 . It should be added that the aforementioned reaction processes may occur concurrently within the same temperature range. Large-capacity LFP and NCM battery exhibit consistent reaction steps, with the exception of varying cathode decomposition processes releasing O2 [93].

Toxicity is a critical consideration in firefighting operations. BVG contains asphyxiating gases such as CO and CO2, as well as the irritant gases like HF and fluoride gases C2H5F [107]. Peng et al. [20] employed FTIR to detect toxic gases like SO2, HCl, NO and NO2 in the BVG of LFP battery, with the peak concentration of HF exceeded the IDLH value (which is directly dangerous to life or health) by an order of magnitude.

To further assess the toxicity of BVG, the toxicity evaluation model outlined in ISO 13571 was employed to evaluate both irritant and asphyxiating gases [100,108]. The toxicity of asphyxiating gases CO and CO2 evaluated using the fractional effective dose concentration (FED), as indicated in equation (1). Irritant gases like SO2, HCl, HF, and NO2 were evaluated using the fractional effective concentration (FEC), as presented in equation (2).

Jia et al. [108] calculated that the FED value of 280 Ah LFP battery exceeds 1 by a significant margin over thousands of seconds. Mao et al. [109] further considered a scenario involving the ignition of 10 ​× ​326 Ah LFP batteries in an 80 ​m3 garage, revealing that the FED exceeded the critical value by two orders of magnitude (Fig. 6 ). Factors affecting the components of BVG will also affect the toxicity of BVG. Bugryniec et al. [110] comprehensively analysis of existing literature, concluding that the toxicity of LFP is greater than that of NCM battery, particularly at low SOC, where LFP toxicity is heightened, whereas the opposite is observed for NCM battery. In addition, toxicity increases in confined spaces [111]. The method of abuse also has a significant impact, with overheated LFP battery being more toxic than overcharged ones, but in overcharging, the FED value with SV on is greater than 1, even if no TR occurs [112].

Understanding the combustion and explosion characteristics of BVG is essential for preventing and mitigating fire accidents in BESS. Flammability limit (FL), laminar flame speed (LFS), laminar burning velocity (LBV), maximum explosion pressure (P max), explosion index (K g), explosion limit (EL) and other sensibility and intensity parameters are utilized to assess the critical conditions for combustion and explosion events in LIBs and their associated hazards. Selecting appropriate research methods can obtain reliable conclusions and reduce experimental costs. This chapter will summarize the combustion and explosion characteristics of BVG based on an overview of experimental and numerical simulation research methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental research on combustion and explosion characteristics

ce:section.ce:para: LFS described as the speed at which a planar flame front propagates through a fuel –air mixture under quiescent conditions [119]. LBV, as the basic combustion characteristic of premixed gases, determines the flame propagation process [120]. Bunsen burner and spherical bombs measurement are the main research methods, in which the shape of the Bunsen flame closely resembles a real flame, but it is prone to heat loss and flame stretching. The Bunsen burner method calculates the LFS according to Fig. 7 a [113,114]. The spherical bombs method can further calculate the LBV based on the calculation LFS [121]. First, the outward propagating spherical flame method is employed to ascertain the flame speed of premixed gas combustion (S b). To determine the unstretched planar LFS, the calculation of the spherical flame stretch rate (k) is needed, then the commonly used linear stretching model (LM-S) can be applied to calculate the LFS and the Markstein length (Fig. 7b) [115]. However, Chen [122] noted out that the linear curvature model (LM-C) is more accurate for gas mixture with L b ​> ​0. In the study of Henriksen et al., presented below, they found that L b ​> ​0 for all BVG composition cases when the equivalence ratio exceeds 0.8 using the LM-S, so the LM-C model is more appropriate (Fig. 7b). On the basis of the above, assuming the flame is quasi-steady and remains continuous, the LBV can be calculated based on Fig. 7c [117].

Bunsen burner experiments demonstrate that the LFS of BVG from various cathode materials surpasses that of CH4. The LFP is significantly higher than that of NCM batteries, with NCM523 is higher than that of NCM811 and NCM622 batteries (Fig. 8 a) [113]. Henriksen et al. [117] calculated the LBV of three exhaust components and simplified premixed gas utilizing the aforementioned spherical explosion vessel measurement method (Fig. 8b). The maximum value of LBV of BVG with high H2, CO content, and low CO2 content reaching 1055 ​mm/s, whereas only 351 ​mm/s, indicating a significant inhibitory effect of CO2 on LBV. In conclusion, augmenting the proportion of H2, CO in BVG will significantly improve the combustion rate. Consequently, during TR, rapid injection of a significant quantity of CO2 into the module may be advisable.

FL includes lower flammable limit (LFL) and upper flammable limit (UFL), and EL includes lower explosive limit (LEL) and upper explosive limit (UEL). These parameters delineate the critical concentration of flammable gas for combustion and explosion, respectively. Given the propensity of BVG to accumulate in confined BESS spaces, both FL and EL should serve as crucial parameters for assessing BESS safety. Yu et al. [126] determined the FL of BVG using the FL meter (HWP21-30S). Wang et al. [113] investigated the EL of BVG for batteries with different cathode materials utilizing a 1 ​L explosive container (Fig. 8d). Additionally, Zhang et al. [127] constructed an in-situ testing platform (electrolyte vapor was measured) and employed the dichotomy method to determine the EL of BVG under different SOC states (Fig. 8d). Nonetheless, due to the higher expenses associated with conducting experiments using various BVG components, mathematical methods can be employed within an acceptable margin of error. Initially, researchers used Le Chatelier's mixing law (L-C formula) to calculate the FL and EL of simplified BVG compositions (Fig. 7e), which requires treating the mixtures as ideal gas [124]. But then, the L-C formula primarily applies to combustible gases like hydrocarbons and does not consider the influence of inert gas components such as CO2 in BVG. Consequently, improved empirical formula was further used to calculate FL and EL (Fig. 7f), incorporating the dilution effect of inert gases [91,118].

BVG is typically generated in non-standard temperature and pressure conditions, yet the aforementioned formulas were applied without accounting for environmental factors. Ma et al. [125] conducted additional research on the impact of initial temperature and pressure on FL (Fig. 8g), subsequently devising an equation correlating FL with initial temperature and pressure (Fig. 7g). It is worth noting that what was used in that the study employed a simplified BVG composition, which possesses inherent limitations. Moreover, some scholars have developed or adopted mathematical methods with reduced errors, providing researchers with a wider array of options as shown in Table 5 .

The findings of the above studies indicate that both NCM and LFP batteries exhibit a wider EL range (ELR) compared to CH4. Notably, LFP battery exhibit the smallest LEL (Fig. 8c), implying a heightened risk of explosion following TR. Conversely, NCA battery possess a broader FL range (FLR) and lower LFL than LFP at low SOC levels (Fig. 8f), which explains why NCA battery are more prone to jet flame. As shown in Fig. 8(d, f), SOC exerts differential effects on EL and FL, with FLR generally increasing with SOC, UFL exhibiting a mild trend of increase followed by decrease, and LFL decreasing rapidly. UEL increases with SOC, while LEL displays a distinctive pattern of increase and subsequent decrease, peaking at 50 ​% SOC. The effect of charging rate on EL displayed a consistent trend, where both LEL and ELR gradually increased with higher charging rate. Additionally, elevated initial temperatures led to decreased LFL and increased UFL, while LFL, UFL, and FLR all exhibited an increase with higher initial pressures (Fig. 8g).

The explosive intensity of BVG can be quantified by the P max and the K g. P max can be directly measured, while K g is derived from the rise rate of the explosion pressure (Fig. 7d) [128]. However, due to the strong influence of the volume and shape of the test apparatus on P max and K
g , results obtained from different instruments are not directly comparable. Under identical experimental conditions, the LFP battery exhibited the most prominent explosion intensity compared with the NCM, featuring P max and K
g twice that of the NCM811 [113]. Furthermore, P max and K
g increased significantly with rising SOC [128].

The aforementioned studies offer initial insights into the explosion intensity of BVG and the influencing factors. Note, however, that the venting process of battery releases a considerable amount of dust particles comprising over 30 elements, including 40 ​% metal particles [129]. Under the “gas-dust two-phase” condition, particles may undergo explosions below the minimum explosive concentration (MEC), and BVG explosions below its LEL value [130]. Furthermore, upon opening the SV, affected by factors airflow and heat transfer uncertainties, the BVG ignition may result in flame folding and the forming turbulent flame [131]. All of the above factors may further enhance the explosion hazard. Wang et al. [132] considered the effects of BVG concentration and particle size. Their findings revealed that within a certain range, increasing BVG concentration led to a decrease in the MEC and an increase in K
g , while the effect of particle size on P max became more pronounced, and P max decreased as particle size decreased. Zhou et al. [131] comprehensively analyzed the effects of dust concentration, initial temperature, and turbulence (Fig. 9 ). Both turbulence and a certain dust concentration can enhance explosion intensity under constant initial temperature conditions. Additionally, while P max decreases with increasing initial temperature at a dust concentration of 25 ​%, K
g continues to increase.

Turbulence enhances the explosion intensity, particularly K
g , by augmenting the HRR and LFS of BVG combustion. Nonetheless, the impact of dust concentration remains ambiguous. In scenarios with lower dust concentrations, the flame ignites the unburned dust, thereby elevating P max and K
g . Conversely, at excessively high concentrations, the unburned dust absorbs heat from the reaction zone due to O2 content constraints, leading to suppression.

Previous research indicates that under high-temperature, low-pressure conditions, the likelihood and intensity of combustion and explosion events during TR are significantly increased in LFP battery experiencing high-rate overcharging, and high-SOC. Importantly, the “barricade excitation” effect may occur, accelerating flame propagation, due to the confined spatial layout of the BESS. Besides, a more dangerous detonation may occur when explosion shockwaves reflect and merge with the incident shockwave, resulting in a bifurcated shock structure [133]. Consequently, the above findings cannot currently be applied to assess the combustion and explosion risks of BVG in BESS.

CHEMKIN and CANTERA have been applied to investigate the combustion and explosion characteristics of BVG. The research process involves selecting a reactor and a reaction kinetics model (incorporating kinetics, thermodynamics, and transport models), as well as defining initial conditions. In particular, the accuracy of results is greatly influenced by the choice of reaction kinetics model. Mathieu et al. [134] analyzed the accuracy of five models in calculating LFS based on experiments, with NUIGMech 1.1 [135] demonstrating the lowest error (Fig. 10 a). However, the effects of CO2 concentration and electrolyte vapor has not yet been considered in the above studies. Henriksen et al. [117]. provided that the GRI 3.0 [136] accurately predicts LBV at low CO2 concentrations (<15 ​%). For CO2 concentrations in the range of 15 ​%–40 ​% and >40 ​%, SanDiegoMech [137] and Sun et al. ‘s DMC model [138] was the most accurate, respectively, suggesting that the BVG components should be fully investigated before choosing a reaction kinetic model. To enhance the model's versatility, Nilsson et al. [139] merged the Alexandrino et al.'s DMC model [140], Sun et al.'s DEC model [141], and Linteris et al.'s HFC combustion model [142]. However, simplification is required if the model is applied to CFD simulations.

This methodology enables a deeper analysis of the production rate of critical radicals, reaction pathways, and sensitivities, providing insight into the reaction mechanisms. Ueda et al. [143] elucidated the mechanism of the SOC impact on LBV (Fig. 10b). Reactions R1 and R27 exhibited the highest positive sensitivity coefficients to LBV. An increase in SOC resulted in higher proportions of H2 and CO, alongside a reduction in CO2 ratio within BVG. Consequently, the molar fractions of ·OH, H·, and CO radicals increased, promoting the reaction rates of R1 and R27, ultimately raising the LBV. Moreover, an increase in SOC can shift the BVG explosion limit curve towards the low-temperature region under high-pressure conditions (Fig. 10c), attributed to the rising percentage of C2H4 with increasing SOC [144]. In high-pressure region, reaction R253, which generates C2H4, replaces reaction R44 and plays a dominant role in promoting the reaction. Reactions R262 and R300, which consume C2H4, begin to predominate in promotion. The sensitivity coefficients of these reactions are all >0, signifying their role in expediting free radical accumulation. Their promotional effects outweighed the inhibition of chain termination reaction R38 (Fig. 10d). Meanwhile, R263 (C2H5O2H (+M) ≤> C2H5O ​+ ​OH (+M)) in the 7th reaction pathway of C2H4 emerges as the pivotal branched chain reaction, yielding a substantial quantity of OH radicals and heightening the likelihood of explosion occurrence (Fig. 10e). Zhang et al.'s [145] work further revealed that in the medium and low-pressure regions, the presence of trace amounts of C2H4 and C2H6 raises the initial temperature threshold for BVG explosion, whereas C2H4 lowers the initial pressure requirement for explosion. These gas components contribute to the non-monotonic variation in BVG explosion limit curve with increasing SOC under medium and low-pressure conditions. Furthermore, a significant research concept arises, suggesting the potential design of LIBs-specific extinguishing agents with a blocking effect for crucial reaction pathways in the chain reaction of BVG explosion, with particular focus on the impact of gas components such as C2H4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CFD simulation for energy storage units of different scales
ce:section.ce:para: CFD simulation has been utilized for evaluating and predicting battery-TR. Wang et al. [146] systematically sorted out the electrical, thermal, mechanical, and fluid dynamics models used to depict the TR evolution in energy storage units across diverse scales. These models are integrated to form a multiphysics coupling model [147], which can be refined for precise depiction of the combustion and explosion traits of energy storage units across all scales. Integrating discrete-phase and continuous-phase venting models in the fluid region (multiphase fluids-solid coupling) facilitates the description of battery-TR incorporating particles diffusion and mass-loss processes (Fig. 11 a) [148]. Coupling the heat transfer among the batteries, flames, and the energy storage chamber elucidates the coupling of various heat transfer pathways while delineating the module TRP process (Fig. 11b) [149]. A coupled network of thermal resistance and mass flow is established in the battery region, and a semi reduced-order model for simulating combustion behavior using a full-order CFD model in the fluid region, allowing for visualization of the flame propagation in a full-size battery energy storage container (BESC) and quantitative analysis of the heat release (Fig. 11c) [150]. These simulations mainly use the open-source software OpenFOAM. Besides, FLACS and FDS have demonstrated distinctive strengths in analyzing BESC explosion and smoke flow, respectively (Fig. 11d and e) [151,152].

CFD simulation contributes to the safety design of battery structure, arrangement, etc. and elucidates phenomena that are challenging to observe experimentally [153]. As for the battery scale, the TR process involves two venting phenomena: particles and electrolyte vapors dominate the first venting, while other combustible gases in the BVG are primarily released during the second venting (Fig. 11a). As for the module scale, greater spatial confinement enhances the promotion of TRP through heat convection of ejected gases and heat radiation from the flame (Fig. 11b) [149]. Also, rapid combustion product transfer between battery gaps accelerates the TRP process, with the SV position exerting a notable influence [154]. As shown in Fig. 11(c, d), the BESC combustion and explosion processes exhibit high-temperatures and HRRs, and the surrounding BESC is more susceptible to high-temperatures within the 0.4 ​m low height layer. Notably, bottom ignition results in higher flame speeds and P max [155]. Furthermore, H2 typically accumulates at the top, with H2 concentration being smaller but more uniformly distributed under ventilated conditions within the container (Fig. 11e), necessitating the addition of top vents to the BESC. Researchers need to notice two additional issues. Firstly, the TRP process is highly randomized, and the potential for jet flames emanating from the side of the battery to trigger TRP is concerning [156]. Secondly, due to computational constraints, the lack of detailed reaction kinetics modeling in combustion modeling may result in significant errors in the results.

Without consideration of the perspective of intrinsic safety, the passive suppression technology centered on BTMS is the first line of defense to prevent TR, mainly including air cooling, liquid cooling, heat pipe cooling, and phase change material cooling [18]. However, the BESS safety incident warns that people still need to focus on active suppression techniques. Building upon earlier discussions, these techniques should possess four critical capabilities: battery cooling, heat transfer blocking, elimination of combustible and toxic gases, and combustion and explosion suppression of BVG to cope with the four hazard stages of battery-TR, module-TRP, BVG-accumulation, and fire and explosion accidents, respectively (Fig. 12 ).

Fine water mist (FWM) possesses attributes such as excellent electrical insulation, efficient dispersion, rapid evaporation, and high latent heat of vaporization [163], enabling suppression efficacy across the four stages delineated in Fig. 12. The first stage is boiling heat transfer. A flowing liquid film will be formed if the FWM is sprayed onto the surface of the battery. As the temperature disparity between T s and the liquid film amplifies, the heat transfer traverses four phases of forced convection, nucleate boiling, transition boiling, and membranous boiling. Bubbles are violently disturbed at the intersection of nucleate boiling and transition boiling, generating numerous evaporation cores with maximum heat flux and cooling efficiency. Upon reaching the Leidenfrost point, stable gas films form on the battery surface, dramatically increasing heat resistance and decreasing cooling efficiency. At this point, the FWM cannot effectively suppress battery heating [164]. Therefore, a critical control temperature for FWM exists at this stage, which is confirmed by experiments on 18650-type LIBs [165,166]. The second stage is heat transfer path blocking. As T s decreases, the temperature gradient between adjacent battery contact surfaces decreases (Q cod decreases), and the liquid film and water vapor on the module surface attenuate Q conv and Q rad between the high-temperature smoke and the battery surface [167,168]. The third and fourth stages involve dilution and physical flame suppression, respectively. Here, FWM vaporizes and absorbs heat in the flame zone, while water vapor mixes with BVG and O2 to mitigate the risk of combustion and explosion [163]. As shown in Table 6 , FWM demonstrates remarkable cooling efficacy before the battery undergoes TR. However, during TR, fire extinguishing efficiency decreases, and cooling ability becomes insufficient. In addition, FWM can react with LiPF6, intensifying HF generation, and HF can be reduced to H2, posing additional risks [169]. In the flame zone, the impact of larger momentum droplets on the flame causes flame oscillation, resulting in enhanced flame instability, This, in turn, results in the fire plume drawing more combustible gases into the combustion zone, leading to a brief intensification of the flame [170].

Particle size significantly influences the fire extinguishing efficiency of FWM, that is, smaller particles have a larger specific surface area and a faster evaporation rate [171]. FWM particles of smaller (around 100 μm) and larger (200 μm) sizes effectively cooled in the early and late stages of TR, respectively [172]. Researchers can modify the spray pressure, duration, and angle to adjust the particle size, droplet momentum, water consumption, and spray range of FWM. This improving the suppression efficacy and avoiding the risk of secondary disasters from excessive water volume [173].

Surfactants and water-soluble inorganic salt additives can align the physical and chemical properties of FWM with the requirements for LIBs fire suppression. Surfactants commonly used include ionic, nonionic and compound types, with ionic surfactants further categorized into anionic, cationic, and amphoteric varieties. Surfactants typically enhance the cooling ability of FWM by reducing the contact angle between droplets and battery surface, improving fluidity, facilitating water penetration into battery gaps, and reducing droplet surface tension as well as particle size [174,175]. Their effectiveness depends on their critical micelle concentration (CMC) and foaming properties. Lower CMC correspond to reduced surfactant quantity [174], while superior foaming properties increase droplet foaming with the interference of air, obstructing the heat transfer [176]. Nonionic surfactants exhibit low CMC and foaming properties, and do not enhance FWM conductivity. Tween 20 (C26H50O10) exhibits superior cooling capacity in FWM compared to cationic surfactant CTAB (C19H42BrN) and anionic surfactant SDS (C12H25SO4Na) [153,155]. Additionally, APG0810 (C16H32O6) exhibits superior cooling capacity compared to Tween 80 (C24H44O6), although the addition of APG0810-1 defoamer to enhance the brittleness of the foam [177]. Notably, the amphoteric surfactant F-500 enhances FWM performance across all four suppression stages with one polar end (hydrophilic) and one non-polar end (lipophilic), which can transfer heat from the non-polar end of F-500 to the polar end, and increase the FWM (with 3 ​% F-500) cooling capacity by 3 times [178]. Moreover, the non-polar end of F-500 can capture the non-polar molecule H2 and the weakly polar molecule CO, and a group of F-500 molecules can perform chemical cocooning on H2 and CO molecules, rendering them non-flammable (Fig. 12c) [161]. FireIce and F-500 have demonstrated a similar effect on hydrocarbon molecules like CH4, yet their higher electrical conductivity raises questions regarding their applicability to BESS, necessitating further investigation [179,180].

The inorganic salt additives integrated into FWM primarily consist of alkali metal salts, phosphates, etc., principally enhancing FWM's suppression efficacy in the fourth stage. Following FWM evaporation, these inorganic salts precipitate and collide with the chain reaction free radicals to exchange energy, and their decomposition byproducts further react with the free radicals to block the chain reaction [175]. Moreover, certain inorganic salt particles (e.g., NaHCO3) undergo pyrolysis to absorb heat while releasing CO2, thereby diluting the BVG concentration, exhibiting physical suppression ability [162]. Interestingly, surfactants' effect of accelerating FWM evaporation can lead to the rapid pyrolysis of the inorganic salt additives in the flame zone, exerting a synergistic suppression function of the two additives, a phenomenon corroborated in experiments [181]. Zhou et al. [182] compounded FWM with nonionic surfactants AEO-9 (C30H62O10) and FC-4430, along with inorganic salt additives urea and DMMP (C3H9O3P), resulting in a solution with conductivity 10 times lower than that of water and a cooling rate more than twice that of pure water.

Compared to pure water, hydrogels exhibit unique coverage cooling abilities and exceptional suppression of LFP module fires [183]. Temperature-sensitive hydrogels deserve further attention. Zhang et al. [184] asserted that temperature-sensitive hydrogels represent an ideal extinguishing agent following a comparative analysis of various patents. These hydrogels exhibit high mobility at lower temperatures, releasing as a liquid to extinguish fires. When the temperature exceeds the low critical solubilization temperature (LCST), phase separation and contraction occur, reducing mobility. Consequently, the hydrogel adheres to the burning material's surface, providing sustained cooling [185]. These hydrogels have proven effective suppression of jetting flame and TRP in 18650-type LIBs modules [186]. However, comprehensive tests evaluating basic physical properties such as water retention, surface tension, LCST, swelling rate, and electrical conductivity have not been conducted extensively in this research area, and the preparation methods do not thoroughly consider the suppression strategy of LIBs fire.

Gaseous fire extinguishing exhibit characteristics of electric insulation, non-corrosive, and easy submergence. CO2, 1,1,1,2,3,3,3-Heptafluoropropane (HFC-227ea, C3HF7) and Dodecafluoro-2-methylpentan-3-one (Novec 1230, C6F12O) absorb heat from the reaction zone due to their own latent heat of vaporization and high heat capacity [180]. Besides that, the decomposition of HFC-227ea in the high-temperature flame zone produces combustion-inhibiting ·CFO, ·CF3 and ·CF2, which react with H·, ·O and ·CH3 radicals in the combustion zone [193]. The reaction of Novec 1230 with OH· and H· results in the formation of ·CF(CF3)2, ·CF2CF3 and ·CF3 etc., which further capture reactive radicals for rapid flame suppression [194]. As shown in Table 7 , among the three mentioned, Novec 1230 exhibits the most effective suppression effect. However, Novec 1230 still faces two prominent issues. Firstly, it is highly concentration-dependent, exhibiting negative combustion suppression at low concentrations [195,196]. Secondly, it can exacerbate CO production through various pathways, significantly increasing systemic toxicity [197].

Liquid nitrogen (LN) may be the ideal gaseous fire extinguishing agent. LN's suppression effect is evident across four hazard stages (Fig. 12). During the initial two stages, the significant temperature differential between LN and the battery surface accelerates the heat transfer process, rapidly transitioning it into a film boiling state. This time the net heat transfer involving both boiling and radiation heat transfer, which continues to reduce the batteries temperature [198]. LN effectively mitigates inter-battery heat conduction, maintaining T s within the TR trigger temperature and swiftly lowers the module's internal temperature to below 20 ​°C, facilitating battery heat dissipation [199]. During the third stage, LN vaporization results in the formation of N2 inside the module, which dilutes the BVG. What's more, the primary gas component responsible for BESS explosion is electrolyte vapors [151], and the low-temperature vapor facilitates it condense rapidly, whose role that has not yet been noticed. During the fourth stage, LN lowers the fuel temperature in the flame zone below the ignition point, while its rapid vaporization creates positive pressure within the module, aiding in asphyxiation suppression [200]. Targeting of the battery-TR trigger position and vertical release method can further enhance the LN suppression capabilities [160,199]. Meanwhile, LN's suppression effectiveness is influenced by spray height and the size of heated surface [201].

Dry powders, known for their long storage period and environmental friendliness, provide suppression through cooling, asphyxiation, and blocking chain reactions [202]. However, the cooling capacity of ABC dry powder is limited (Table 7). To address this, Li et al. [203] investigated the suppression effect of AASD powder (NH4Al(SO4)2⋅12H2O) and found that its heat absorption capacity was more than 10 times that of Novec 1230, and the pyrolysis product of the composite component NH4H2PO4 (ADP) effectively blocked chain reactions, suppressing both jet flame and TRP in 18650-type LIBs module.

In recent years, multiphase extinguishing agents have attracted significant attention from researchers. Hot aerosols, which are technically mature, were among the first to be tested due to their high extinguishing efficiency and minimal space requirements [208]. It consists of 40 ​% solid components and 60 ​% inert gases, in which the suppression mechanism of the solid components is the same as chemical suppression. Furthermore, it's tiny particles (about 1 μm) can exert non-homogeneous chemical suppression by adsorbing chain-reaction free radicals and catalyzing their reorganization into stable molecules [209]. However, the suppression effect of hot aerosols remains unsatisfactory (Table 7), and no relevant research reports on cooled aerosol fire suppression techniques have been retrieved.

Liquid-containing multiphase extinguishing agents have promising application potential. Dry water (DW) is a novel core-shell composite material with hydrophobic SiO2 as the shell and water as the core material. The shell limits the evaporation of water in the flame zone, enhancing its ability to cool combustibles [210]. The heat-absorbing capacity of DW is 80 ∼ 94 ​% of that of liquid water, and it reaches peaking around 150 ​°C, which can effectively suppress the TRP of 18650-type LIBs [211]. Wang et al. [212] found that 8 ​% ADP-modified DW had the excellent suppression effect on LIBs fire compared to urea and Mg(OH)2 ​at an optimal release pressure of 0.4 ​MPa. However, DW has drawbacks such as low bulk density, poor compression resistance, and poor moisture retention. In this regard, researchers have developed inorganic salt-modified gel DW to improve pressure resistance, moisture retention, and extinguishing ability, but it has not yet been applied to LIBs fire [213–215]. Furthermore, Fig. 13 a [216] shows a CTS-SA@F7A-Novec 1230 microcapsule with chitosan (CTS) and sodium alginate (SA) as the shell, and liquid heptafluorocyclopentane (F7A) and Novec 1230 as the core. The three-phase extinguishing agent composed of this microcapsule, N2 and ABC can play the synergistic suppression of gas-liquid-solid extinguishing agent (Fig. 13b), which is effective in suppressing the flame of 18650-type LIBs, but its cooling ability needs improvement (Fig. 13c).

As previously stated, many of the new extinguishing agents introduced in section 5.1 achieved better results in the 18650-type LIBs fire experiments. However, their suppression effects on BESS fires remain unclear and warrant further investigation. Additionally, the limitations of storage space may adversely impact the actual application effectiveness of these extinguishing agents, posing a significant challenge for fire suppression systems.

Fire suppression strategies for BESS are primarily of two types: total submergence of the BESS and localized application to specific modules. The former involves injecting a large amount of gas or water into the BESS following an early warning system alert to achieve widespread suppression. However, injecting water may cause ISC [217]. The latter enables precise suppression at the modules where TR occurs, thereby protecting healthy batteries. This section aims to analyze the selection of extinguishing agents and summarize the suppression strategies.

The suppression capability of an extinguishing agent is the primary selection criterion, and should also be weighed against various factors such as environmental impact, cost, risk of secondary disasters, and application scenarios [218]. Synthesized as described in subsection 5.1 and Fig. 14 . Commonly used agents like Novec 1230 and FWM exhibit outstanding extinguishing and cooling capabilities, respectively. F-500-containing FWM and LN demonstrate superior cooling, fire suppression, and combustible gas removal capabilities, making them suitable for addressing the four hazard stages. Nevertheless, the poor insulation properties of F-500-containing FWM limit their use in BESS. It is worth noting that in the BVG accumulation stage, inert gases like CO2 and N2 can significantly reduce the FLR and LFS of the BVG (Fig. 14d) and can rapidly achieve total flooding, thereby mitigating combustion and explosion hazards [126]. Therefore, LN may be the optimal choice, and the synergistic use of gas and water-based extinguishing agents can likewise achieve full suppression.

The objective of the gas-liquid synergistic suppression strategy is to enhance the suppression effect. This strategy can be classified into distributed synergy and integrated synergy based on the method of extinguishing agent deployment.

Distributed synergy entails spraying the extinguishing agent into the fire through independent pipelines and nozzles (Fig. 15a1 ). Det Norske Veritas (DNV) [220] recommends initiating with the spraying of gaseous extinguishing agents to extinguish the open flame, followed by the application of water-based extinguishing agents to sustain continuous cooling. Fire suppression experiments conducted by Zhang et al. [221] on 243 Ah LFP battery demonstrated that the synergistic suppression effect of Novec1230 and FWM surpassed that of the single-phase extinguishing method, and the combination of CO2 and FWM proved to be more cost-effective (Fig. 15b). A shorter time interval between the release of Novec1230 and FWM yielded superior suppression outcomes (Fig. 15a). This is attributed to Novec1230's ability to disperse the smoke plume, facilitating FWM penetration through the plume to reach the battery surface. Conversely, with an increased time interval, a stable carbon-rich smoke plume gradually forms above the battery [222].

Integrated synergy involves connecting the fire extinguishing pipelines to the twin-fluid nozzle, where the gas extinguishing agent atomizes the water-based extinguishing agent at the nozzle before jointly spraying it into the fire (Fig. 15c1). As the air pressure increases, water droplets become susceptible to being blown away by airflow, leading to a decrease in the impact density of FWM on the surface. However, the airflow can expel air bubbles in the liquid film, enhancing the heat transfer and thereby increasing the cooling capacity of FWM within a certain air pressure range [223]. N2-twin-fluid FWM (NWM) demonstrates the capability to swiftly extinguish flames, prevent re-ignition, and elevate the battery surface's cooling rate by over 20 ​% [224]. Jiang et al. [225] considered module space constraints by positioning the nozzle on the side and leveraging the siphon principle to use N2 to transport water to the nozzle, thereby saving space occupied by the pump (Fig. 15c1). As shown in Fig. 15(c2, c3), the cooling capacity of NWM is linked to the ratio of water mist to the volumetric flow rate of N2, exhibiting a trend resembling Gaussian curves. A 0.5 ​MPa NWM suppression is the optimal, maximizing heat dissipation from the batteries while minimizing mass loss. Furthermore, this synergistic approach significantly conserves water consumption, safeguards the battery, and reduces CO generation [226].

Intermittent spray (IS) refers to a spray system regulated at specific frequencies and open periods, facilitating heat absorption through the phase change of the extinguishing agent [228], This method enhances cooling efficiency by adjusting parameters such as the mass flow rate, open period (P t, comprising the single spray time and interval time), and duty cycle (DC, the single spray time as a percentage of the cycle). For instance, when P t ​= ​45 ​s and DC ​≈ ​0.9, LN can suppress the TR process of 65 Ah LFP battery, exhibiting an effective heat absorption rate approximately 10 ​% higher than continuous spray (CS) [206]. The efficiency enhancement mechanism of IS, as elucidated by Zhang et al. [159,227], involves the rapid thinning and momentum loss of the liquid film on the battery surface during the interval time, leading to increased evaporation rates and facilitating bubble overflow in the liquid film. At this juncture, the cooling power (q c) surpasses the battery heating power (q h). However, excessively prolonged interval times result in complete evaporation of the liquid film, tipping the balance in favor of q h, leading to temperature rebounds (Fig. 15d). In terms of suppression efficacy, the cooling effect of IS heightens with the decrease of P t, initially rising and then falling with decreasing DC. Combining various IS strategies can effectively extinguish fires and block TRP (Fig. 15e).

From the extensive body of above previous work, three promising active suppression technology schemes have emerged: (1) LN ​+ ​intermittent spray strategy, (2) a synergistic scheme combining C6F12O6/CO2 with additive-containing FWM, and (3) a multiphase extinguishing agent coupled with dilution, inerting, cooling, and chemical suppression. However, these schemes still face challenges related to the high costs of preparation, use, and maintenance of extinguishing agents, as well as the large space they occupy, hindering their promotion in BESS.

This work offers a comprehensive overview of the hazard characteristics associated with LIBs for energy storage and evaluates the effectiveness of active suppression techniques, leading to the following conclusions:
(1)
Large-capacity LIBs exhibit a low TR trigger temperature with an opposing peak temperature, accompanied by significant toxicity, combustion, and explosion risks in BVG. Overcharging poses an extreme hazard, elevating the likelihood of TR occurrence, especially with a high charging rate, leading to a substantial increase in the proportion of H2 and CO in BVG and exacerbating combustion and explosion risks.
(2)
Spatial characteristics primarily influence the heat transfer mechanisms during the TRP process, which accelerates with increasing spatial constraints. Among other influencing factors, the effects of cathode material and SOC are significant, while the toxicity and LEL of BVG do not exhibit a monotonic relationship with SOC increase.
(3)
Chemical kinetics simulation of BVG combustion and explosion elucidate the influence mechanisms of SOC and gas composition. Full-scale CFD simulation of diverse energy storage units quantify and visualize hazardous processes, providing valuable insights for the design of extinguishing agents and BESS safety.
(4)
LN can effectively suppress fires in all four hazard stages with minimal harm to the battery and no risk of secondary disasters. Additionally, the gas-liquid synergistic strategy also demonstrates outstanding suppression effects, and the cooling efficiency of extinguishing agents can be further enhanced through intermittent spraying techniques. However, there is no recognized solution to the shortcomings of the active suppression techniques described above.

ce:list-item.ce:para: When designing safety protection technology and management measures for BESS, it is imperative to meticulously consider the hazardous characteristics and associated influencing factors. This ensures the safety of BESS across a broader range of scenarios, which is a pivotal aspect of the information presented in this work. Even for SEBs with improved safety, the same evaluation indices can be used to study and enhance their intrinsic safety, facilitating their commercialization and promotion.

There remains a need for in-depth exploration of BVG combustion and explosion characteristics. Presently, existing experimental measurement methods do not fully match with observed accident phenomena. Researchers can address this gap by simulating the structure of the BESC to construct a scaled-down combustion and explosion experimental platform. This would facilitate a comprehensive study of potential flame acceleration mechanisms and provide more accurate evaluations of BVG combustion and explosion characteristics. Consequently, this would enable a thorough assessment of existing safety design schemes and the efficacy of active suppression technologies.

The development of active suppression technologies enables rapid control at each hazard stage remains essential. Researchers must comprehensively evaluate the suppression effects of existing agents and develop new ones, particularly multiphase media that offer synergistic suppression, based on the characteristics of each hazard stage and the properties of the energy storage space. Summarizing the above discussions, we recommend integrating fire suppression systems with early warning systems in large-scale BESS. Meanwhile, designing hierarchical fire suppression strategies based on the evolution of hazard stages and the advantages of various extinguishing agents will offer effective and diversified options for complex BESS application scenarios and the control needs of different hazard stages.

ce:section.ce:para: In the near future, LIBs will continue to be the primary energy storage carriers in BESS, and their safety remains a significant concern. While the large-scale application of SEBs holds promise, as discussed in subsection 2.3, their TR hazards require further in-depth research. Additionally, SEBs can also burn at high temperatures, producing toxic gases such as SO2 and CO2 [229,230]. Therefore, the development of their safety systems should not be overlooked. We believe that BESS security can be effectively guaranteed with the concerted efforts of a wide range of researchers.
